title: ExoMol Line Lists -- LXII: Ro-Vibrational Energy Levels and \\ Line-Strengths for the Propadienediylidene () in its Ground Electronic State

author_details.name: A. E. Lynas-Gray
affiliations: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University College London, Gower Street, London WC1E 6BT, UK

author_details.name: O. L. Polyansky
affiliations: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University College London, Gower Street, London WC1E 6BT, UK

author_details.name: J. Tennyson
affiliations: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University College London, Gower Street, London WC1E 6BT, UK

author_details.name: S. N. Yurchenko
affiliations: Department of Physics and Astronomy, University College London, Gower Street, London WC1E 6BT, UK

author_details.name: N. F. Zobov
affiliations: Institute of Applied Physics, Russian Academy of Science, Ulyanov Street 46, Nizhnii Novgorod 603950, Russia

journal: Monthly Notices of the Royal Astronomical Society
abstract: Improved opacities are needed for modelling the atmospheres and evolution of cool carbon-rich stars and extra-solar planets; in particular, contributions made by the astrophysically important propadienediylidene ( ) molecule need, at a minimum, to be determined using a line list which includes all significant transitions in the energy range of interest. We report variational calculations giving ro-vibrational energy levels and corresponding line-strengths for , \ and . In the \ case we obtain 2 166 503 ro-vibrational state energies \ for the electronic ground-state. Comparison with experiment indicates a maximum error of \ in calculated positions of lines involving an upper state energy . For lines with upper state energies \ to have comparable line-position accuracies, conical intersections would need to be accounted for in an adopted potential energy surface. Line lists and associated opacities are provided in the ExoMol Database (http://www.exomol.com). \\
body_text:
-
 

 

 

 0.3cm 

Introduction

Interest in propadienediylidene (tricarbon, hereinafter )
follows the demonstration by that the
molecular band near 4050 \ in cometary spectra may be
attributable to it. First observed by ,
the 4050 \ band is also seen in carbon star spectra
 and work by

allowed to identify \ as the carrier.
A detection of \ is to be expected as
's 
determination of the dissociation energy (the energy required to
break a C-C bond) of 160 kcal/mol (6.938 eV) led
 to demonstrate that both
 \ and \ achieve maximum number densities in conditions
prevailing in some carbon star atmospheres at temperatures
 K.

 reproduced the 4050 \ band in
fluorescence and absorption using flash photolysis of diazomethane
mixed with an excess (100:1) of nitrogen. The electronic transition
 
was found to produce the
 4050 \ band. Following their rotational analysis,
 established through a vibrational
analysis that there are two low lying levels
at 132 and 286 which have to be
respectively assigned as and , leading to the
discovery of the surprisingly low-order bending mode
 .

 exploited the \ low-order bending mode
to establish its presence in the interstellar medium along the
line-of-sight towards Sgr B2(M). Reactions involving \ in
the interstellar medium are understood to determine carbon chain
abundances in dark clouds . Because \
and \ are obvious building blocks for larger carbon molecules
such as , located \ in a
further twenty-seven lines-of-sight for follow-up observations.

 revised -hydrocarbon models of
Titan's atmosphere, finding , \ and \ may be
detectable. Results from high dispersion optical spectroscopy
 are consistent with a \ column
density of in the upper atmosphere of Titan.
An updated line list enabled to detect
propadiene
( )
in the atmosphere of Titan using infrared high dispersion spectra;
their spectra could also be used to search for \ were an
adequate line list available.

 reviewed R Corona Borealis (RCrB) stars whose atmospheric
compositions are 99\% helium and 1\% carbon (by numbers); their light
curves exhibit sharp and irregular decreases in brightness of roughly
6-8 magnitudes, the original magnitude being recovered only after a
slow increase in brightness which follows. Soot formation and
dissipation in RCrB atmospheres is understood to be the cause of the
characteristic RCrB light-curves and it was therefore of some interest
to note that laboratory studies of \ show it to be the dominant
vapour species over graphite above K and identify it as a
potential precursor for soot formation in flames
. identify the
4050 \ \ band in a RCrB spectrum obtained at maximum light
and find it to have disappeared as minimum light is approached,
suggesting that \ is now absent and soot formation has taken
place. 

Experiments by and
 respectively located the \ ground state
asymmetric and symmetric stretches at 2040 and
1230 . then argued that
 \ should be included as a contributor to a broad absorption feature seen
in carbon star spectra at and that it contributes significant
opacity at this frequency.
 studied the vibrational
spectrum of \ in the region, using a carbon
tube furnace at 3100 K; they found a broad unresolved feature at
 which is 
wide and unambiguously identified with the asymmetric stretch
ro-vibrational band of .

 compared phase-dependent observed and
synthetic carbon star spectra, the calculations being based on
dynamical model stellar atmospheres by .
Of specific interest was the comparison with the \
 feature which 
had difficulty in modelling. Among possible explanations in need of
investigation is 's use of opacities
from the database which in the \ case
are based on the line list by .
 present an ExoMol
 atlas of molecular opacities and
note that by modern standards
the \ line list cannot be
considered reliable, given improved methods and routine tuning of
calculated line-frequencies to experimental data.

 published a global \ potential energy surface
(PES) obtained using the MRCI/AVTZ//FVCAS/AVTZ level of theory, which correctly describes the overall topology of the \
electronic ground state; this includes the all important conical
intersections. Quite good agreement is achieved for pure
bending modes but the stretching modes are not calculated with the
accuracy required for a high quality line list.
However, calculated an accurate local
(near equilibrium) \ PES using the fc-CCSD(T*)-F12b/aug-cc-pV5Z level of theory which reproduces all
then available rotational-vibrational term energies to better than 1 ; they achieve this by taking special care with potential energy
convergence relative to high-order correlation effects,
core-variance correlation, basis set size and scalar relativity.
Moreover, the rotational constants exhibit relative errors of not more
than 0.01\%. Regardless of a decent \ quality, this is still not sufficiently accurate for line list applications. We have therefore constructed a new PES for C3 by refining an \ PES to experimentally derived ro-vibrational energies by and chosen the 
PES as a starting point for these refinements. 

 reported an \ electric dipole moment
surface (EDMF) of \ using the fc-CCSD(T*)-F12b/AV5Z level of theory which we adopt in our line list intensity calculations. 
Note that this allows us to provide absolute transition intensities for all transitions that we consider,
something missing from all experimental studies reported up until now.

 summarised progress made with the ExoMol
project up to April 2024, and the
provision of a database from which line lists and associated data
may be retrieved. Examples of available line lists include alkali metal hydroxides [KOH and NaOH,][] 2021MNRAS.502.1128O ,
silicon monoxide , calcium and magnesium
monohydride [CaH and MgH,][] 2022MNRAS.511.5448O ,
silicon mononitrate [SiN,][] 2022MNRAS.516.1158S ,
calcium monohydroxide [CaOH,][] 2022MNRAS.516.3995O ,
 isotopologues [ , ,
 and ,][] 2023MNRAS.519.6333B ,
thioformaldehyde ,
aluminium monochloride [AlCl,][] 2023MNRAS.520.5183Y ,
lithium hydroxide [LiOH,][] 2024MNRAS.527..731O ,
yttrium oxide [YO,][] 2024MNRAS.527.4899Y ,
sulphur monoxide [SO,][] 2024MNRAS.527.6675B ,
aluminium hydride and aluminium deuteride [AlH and AlD,][]
 2024MNRAS.527.9736Y and the
methylidene cation [ ,][] 2024MNRAS.52710726P .

Reported in the present paper are calculations which produce ExoMol
database line lists for , \ and ; the choice of isotopologues having been
influenced by the detection by of \ and
 \ in the direction of Sgr B2(M). 
mention an earlier failure to find interstellar \ using the
Herschel space telescope, assuming a lowest bending mode of
60.747 for \ as reported.
Laboratory experiments by identified the
 absorption as belong to ; as this was not observed
we concluded that there was no immediate need for isotopolgue
line lists where more than one has been substituted by .

The summary of early work on \ presented above is necessarily
brief and focussed on astronomical spectroscopy. For more details,
including laboratory studies, see and
.

 

 

Ro-vibrational calculations

 published the variational methodology and associated program (TROVE) for general 
calculations of ro-vibrational energies for an arbitrary small or medium-sized polyatomic. For triatomics, TROVE can use an exact kinetic energy operator (KEO) as developed by for quasi-linear molecules, adopted here for our \ line list calculations using the PES and EDMF discussed below. We follow closely the calculation procedure described in detail by , where TROVE was used to compute a ro-vibrational line list for CO2. The KEO is built using the bisector frame and valence coordinates , (bond lengths) and , where is a bond angle.

TROVE uses numerically constructed 1D primitive basis functions optimised for a given PES. The stretching basis functions and are generated using a Numerov-Cooley procedure , while the bending basis functions are obtained by solving the corresponding Schr\" o dinger equation using associated Laguerre polynomials, see . For , the basis set was limited by the following conditions
 
and , and . 
Extra bending functions are needed to allow for the low frequency of the bending mode and the large range of angles (approaching 90 ) sampled
by the excited bending states we consider here.
The primitive basis functions are then further improved through a two-step contraction procedure. The final ro-vibrational basis functions are formed as symmetrically adapted products (see for details)
 
where is an eigenfunction of the vibrational ( ) Schr\" o dinger equation and is a symmetry adapted (Wang) rigid rotor wavefunction with as its parity. 
 Here corresponds to the maximal value of the rotational quantum number (projection of the rotational angular momentum on the molecular axis ) in the ro-vibrational basis .
In Eq. (), and are the total and vibrational basis symmetries, respectively. For the two symmetric isotopologues \ and , the C2v(M) molecular symmetry group is used to classify the ro-vibrational eigen-solutions spanning four irreducible representations , , and . 
For the asymmetric isotopologue , the Cs(M) molecular symmetry group is used for the final ro-vibrational states. 
Internally, we employed the so-called artificial extended molecular symmetry group Cnv(AEM) to classify the rotational and bending basis functions, which helped reduce the memory requirement.

Potential Energy and Dipole Moment Surfaces

 extracted observed transition wavenumbers
within and between the 
and \ states from twenty-one
publications, subjecting these to a Measured Active
Rotational-Vibrational Energy Levels
 [MARVEL,][] 2007JMoSp.245..115F,2024JQSRT.31608902T analysis which yields 1887
empirical energy levels. We refined the
 PES using those
 corrected energy
levels lists in his table 3,
with the exception of those at 4081.9, 4199.9, 4333.9 and 4486.7 \ for
which the energy uncertainties were comparatively high at
0.5 .

PES refinement followed the procedure described in detail by and used for many ExoMol line list calculations, including the very recent works on H2CS , CH4 , OCS and N2O . 
The equilibrium bond length and angle adopted were those use. Following , our refined PES was also constrained to the PES at geometries not represented by MARVEL corrected state energies used in the PES refinement.
For the refinements we selected 276 ro-vibrational MARVEL term values for , 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 with low uncertainties ( ). These were reproduced with a root-mean-squares (rms) of 0.026 .

The quality of the fit is illustrated in Table , where we show TROVE predictions for 21 states compared to MARVEL energy levels for the \ state [his table 3] jt915 . 's assignments are also replicated in Table : - symmetric stretch, - bend, - projection of on to the bond angle bisector, - asymmetric stretch, - rotational quantum number and - rotationless parity ( even or odd). MARVEL energy uncertainties were placed in parentheses and give 's estimate of the error in the least significant digits provided. TROVE and MARVEL state energies were in agreement, even at 8800 \, to within experimental error in the latter; this was unexpected given the presence of conical intersections to be discussed below. Table residuals have a mean of . 

 construct their electric dipole moment surface (EDMF) from 372 symmetry-unique nuclear configurations. Local transformation to the Eckart coordinate system yields parallel and perpendicular components. Least squares fits gave
 analytical expressions for the two components which we adopted for our line list calculations.

To summarise, our spectroscopic model consists of a refined PES and an \ DMS of C3, in its ground electronic state. We do not take into account any conical intersections or other couplings with other electronic states, see, e.g. , nor any pre-dissociative effects - C3 has a high dissociation energy, eV .

Line list Calculations

Table lists TROVE input parameters selected,
as well as numbers of states and transitions calculated in each
case. 
Adopted carbon atom masses were 11.996709 and 13.00006335 Da; these are nuclear masses, with
core valence electron masses added.

Transitions included in our line lists were limited to those for which the upper state energy is less than \ and the lower state energy is less than . A lower state energy maximum of 10000 \ was selected in order to provide the sufficient temperature coverage for \ absorption spectra simulations, while the upper energy thresholds is to maintain the line list completeness for the wavenumber range of 0-10000 . Rotation quantum number upper limits were matched to the lower state energy threshold, above which no further population of ro-vibrational states were considered. 

State energies in \ are slightly lower than corresponding state energies 
in , resulting in more being selected below a given maximum state energy, 
leading to more transitions being calculated in the \ case. 

A much larger number of transitions calculated for the \ isotopologue is a consequence of 
more parity-allowed transitions in the case of an asymmetric molecule. Indeed, only even-parity 
states ( and ) exist for \ and \ due to zero nuclear spin of 12C 
and the Pauli principle, while the asymmetric species \ has no such restrictions 
and all states are present. 

The line lists are provided in the ExoMol format , which consists of

a two file-type set, a states file and transitions files, augmented by a partition function.
The states file for \ contains the ro-vibrational energy term values, 
quantum numbers, life times, uncertainties and state degeneracies. Each state is 
labeled with a state ID, a counting number. The structure of the \ states file 
is illustrated in Table where we show an extract from the "AtLast"\ line 
list for . We note that while the quantum number labels given for levels labelled
'Ma' for MARVELised can be regarded as secure; the quantum labels for other levels
are automatically generated by TROVE and represent only best estimates.

Transitions files contain Einstein A-coefficients and upper/lower state 
IDs and divided into 10 files in 1000 cm-1 ranges. An extract from a \ transition 
file is given in Table . We used the \ MARVEL energies of 
 to replace the calculated values where available. 
These entries are indicated in the states file with the label 'Ma'. 
The coverage of the MARVELised data is illustrated in Fig. , 
where we show a room temperature ( K) spectrum of \ and the MARVELised 
lines indicated with the red circles. At this temperature, there are 16 178 MARVELised 
transitions which provide the experimental accuracy as based on the the experimental 
uncertainties of the MARVEL dataset by .
These high accuracy transitions can be accessed via the new ExoMolHR web app .

 

 

 

 0.5cm

Cross-Section, Partition Function and Opacity Estimates

 provided a general utility program (ExoCross) for processing transition and states files generated by TROVE and other programs. ExoCross was used to calculate cross-sections and partition functions based on transitions and corresponding ro-vibrational states calculated with TROVE
for the electronic ground-state of . 

 

 

Figure shows derived cross-sections,
at K (red curve) and K (black curve),
for the \ electronic ground state 
sampled at 1 \ intervals and
convolved with a Gaussian of half-width at
half-maximum (HWHM) of 1 . Of particular note is the peak near
2000 which was understood to correspond with the laboratory
measurement by [their figure 2] 1975ApJ...202..839T .
The weaker band at 3300 \ can also be identified.

Cross-sections for all three isotopologues were similarly
calculated for electronic ground states at K,
sampled at 1 \ intervals and convolved
with Gaussians of HWHM = 1 \ and plotted in Figure . The top display, where cross-sections are scaled by relative \ and \
solar abundances, illustrates the relative importance of different isotopologues of C3, while the bottom display, where the un-scaled cross-sections are overlaid, illustrates the isotope frequency shift between , \ and , which is discernable and can be seen even at low resolution in Figure . The consequences for the computed transition frequencies when a replaces a atom were more obvious in Figure and Figure where the 2040 \ and 3300 \ bands for all three isotopologues are plotted, showing differences of about 50 . 

 

 

Temperature-dependent partition functions were obtained as Boltzmann sums over all states in a line list:
 
where is the energy term value for state ( ) calculated with TROVE for the electronic ground-state of , is the corresponding total angular momentum, is the second radiation constant (cm/K), is the state dependent nuclear spin degeneracy and is the temperature (K). Since our partition functions only include states from the ground electronic states and therefore are not complete, especially at high temperatures, they 
thus represent lower limits.

Note that ExoMol uses the HITRAN convention for partition functions which include the full nuclear spin degeneracy. The partition functions for , \ and \ were computed using temperatures in the range 1 to 5000 K in 1 K steps using ExoCross, which calculates
a finite sum over all calculated states following (for example) the approach which adopt. The resulting dependence of partition function with temperature was plotted in Figure . Given energy maxima and the -range selected (Table ), partition functions plotted in Figure 
were fully converged. 's polynomial coefficients lead to a \ partition function lower than our estimate by roughly one order of magnitude; this was anticipated as his polynomial coefficients are based on studies by and , predating pioneering work by who demonstrated the importance of the contribution from the low frequency bending mode. Since 12C has zero nuclear spin, there is no difference for , but for the -containing isotopologues considered here, our partition functions will be a factor of two larger than standard astrophysical ones which neglect the nuclear spin factor.

 

Apart from the states, transition and partition function files, ExoMol line list 
compilations contain opacities and line broadening parameters. Opacities in the form of
cross-sections and k-tables 
were calculated following the procedure describe,
adopting their temperature and pressure grid.
Molecular opacities for C3 are provided in four files, each formatted for 
the exoplanet atmospheric retrieval code identified in the file name: 
ARCiS , TauREx , NEMESIS and 
petitRADTRANS .

As [their figure 1] 1984A+A...132..236B show, an accurate
determination of the microturbulent velocity is required for a solar
abundance determination based on several absorption lines, 
using precision oscillator strengths and a one-dimensional static model
stellar atmosphere in which radiation transfer occurs only along an 
observer's line-of-sight.
 use more realistic dynamical model 
atmospheres, in which three-dimensional radiation transfer is coupled
with the convective velocity field; they show that a
microturbulent velocity is an artefact of the static one-dimensional 
model approximation and not needed. Retrieval codes for which
we have provided \ opacities make no use of microturbulent
velocity and we have accordingly ignored it in our opacity table
calculations. 

Comparison with Experiment

 [their figure 5] 2023JMoSp.39111734M present a Fourier
transform infrared spectrum of \ in absorption from 1900 to
2100 , deducing a 700 K rotational temperature and a Gaussian
line-shape of width 0.005 \ as their experimental conditions.
Computed \ transitions and state energies were used with ExoCross to synthesise the spectrum, assuming the
same temperature and Gaussian width. The comparison is shown in
Figure : black is used for the
 spectrum and red for the spectrum
synthesised using \ transitions and energies reported in the
present paper. The wavenumber range plotted in Figure 
corresponds to the \ broad absorption feature
plotted in Figs. and .

 

A high resolution inspection of individual lines plotted in
Figure showed a maximum difference between observed and calculated line-positions of 0.03 , as expected given the formal least squares errors obtained using MARVEL energies for PES refinement described above. In most cases, calculated and observed line-positions agreed to better than 0.01 \ as indicated by the state energy
comparison presented in Table .
Our calculated line-intensities are also in good agreement with the observed relative values,
as may be seen in Figure , showing the band structure to
have been correctly calculated. Our values can be used to place observations on an absolute scale.

 1pt 

 

Best fit molecular vibrational term values (band centres) by
 [their tables 3, 5 and 7] 2013JPCA..117.3332K for , \ and \ 
are listed in Table (Column 3) for comparison with our ( ) energies of . 
The \ \ energy term value in Column 4 was taken from
 [their table 2] 2003ZNatA..58..129G and the \ \ value in the
same column was a refit to the measurements of by
 [their table 7] 2013JPCA..117.3332K . With the exception of the
 \ \ entry where the difference was larger than 0.8 , satisfactory
agreement is obtained. This is especially reassuring considering that our model (PES) was optimised for the main isotopologue only. 

 4pt 

 

 

 

 

 [their table 3] 2016JChPh.145w4302B use a terahertz-supersonic jet spectrometer,
in combination with a laser ablation source, to measure lowest
bending mode transition frequencies of linear \ and its
 -substituted isotopologues. Table 
compares the lowest order bending mode transition frequencies
for , \ and \ extracted from our line lists with the
 measurements. In the \ case,
calculated frequencies agree with experiment to 0.0005 \ in the
worst case ( , R-Branch); this was understood to be a consequence
of MARVEL corrections having been applied and PES improvements that these
made possible. The \ \ frequency

deduce by fitting molecular parameters was noted above as differing from
our states file entry by more than 0.8 ; this was reduced to 0.0005 \
when the [their table 3] 2016JChPh.145w4302B 
measurement was adopted, as shown in Table .

Line lists for \ and \ have been prepared without MARVEL corrections
as, to the best of our knowledge, necessary experimental data do not currently
exist. As a result, the Table comparison with experiment
for -substituted isotopologues was less satisfactory.
We also noted that use of a PES refined for \
would have been non-optimum for \ and \ line list calculations.
While lowest bending mode transition frequency residuals for \
were found to be always less than 0.03 \, they were all positive and
therefore indicative of an underlying systematic error.

 conduct infrared 
high resolution spectroscopy of , also using a supersonic jet
and a laser ablation source, in combination
with a continuous-wave parametric oscillator as a radiation source.
In Table we have compared our wavenumbers for
 transitions with those
 [their tables 1, 4 and 6] 2013JPCA..117.3332K measure.
In the \ case, calculated frequencies agree with experiment to
0.0018 \ in the worst case ( , R-Branch); this was again
understood to be a consequence of MARVEL corrections having been applied
and PES improvements that these made possible.

As with the comparison, the Table 
comparison with experiment for -substituted isotopologues was
less satisfactory. The absence of negative residuals is again apparent
and indicative of an underlying systematic error
in our \ and \ calculations. Identifying the origin of the apparent
systematic error in our \ and \ line list calculations was regarded
as beyond the scope of the present study but an obvious first step would
be to test the validity of the PES used, especially as this had been carefully
refined to optimise \ calculations.

Discussion

 showed experimentally that the
 \ broad absorption feature completely dominates
the integrated \ cross-section at 3100 K, with transitions
calculated in the present paper showing that this extends downwards to
temperatures of 300 K. The \ band was therefore
found to completely dominate the infrared \ opacity as
 suggests. As \ transitions
computed in the present paper reproduce the \
band observe, it was clear that our
line lists could be used to calculate the \ contribution to
molecular opacity at temperatures less than K; this limit
is a consequence of the fact that our lower state energies were 

limited to states below 10000 .

 provide low-temperature gas
opacities. In the \ case, 
estimate this contribution using the line list by
. Our \ line list is complete to
transition frequencies of 10000 \ or less and our associated states
file complete for electronic ground state ro-vibrational energies less
than 20000 ; it is therefore an improvement on the earlier
line list by , especially as our
calculated stretching frequencies are in very much better agreement with
experimental determinations. An immediate application of our line lists
for \ and its -substituted isotopologues would be
to update low-temperature gas
opacities. As part of this work, a similar update has been applied to the ExoMolOP Database which did not include a \ contribution.

Use of high-dispersion transit spectroscopy to detect and characterise
extra-solar planets, by for example,
requires line lists for individual molecules having accurate
line-positions and relative intensities. Our \ line list was
understood to be suitable for high-dispersion transit spectroscopy at
wavelengths longer than 
( ). Making our \ line list
suitable for high-dispersion transit spectroscopy at shorter wavelengths
would be complicated by the presence of conical intersections as
, and
 review. Briefly, in the Born-Oppenheimer
approximation for molecular dynamics, nuclei move on potential energy
surfaces corresponding to associated electronic states; these may
intersect at a point in nuclear coordinates with the topology of a
double cone. While improve on the PES by
 and achieve an accurate representation in
the vicinity of the four conical intersections, they do not claim an
improvement for other geometries. Further PES development work will
therefore be needed.

As note, the 4050 \ line cannot
be used to detect \ in the interstellar medium and cool stars
through the lack of ultraviolet flux; they identify this molecule
in the circumstellar shell surrounding the obscured carbon star
IRC using observed line positions from which molecular constants
were deduced. Detection of , where it exits, is important as
chemical models of the environment are then constrained and our \
line list should facilitate searches when infrared or radio data
are available. A kinematic analysis using lines of molecular species,
identified and for which number densities are known through a reliable
chemical model, establishes star formation modes as
 demonstrate.

As it is understood from stellar evolution calculations 
 that will be enriched 
in stars ascending the giant and asymptotic giant branches due to dredge-up,
and detect \ and \ in the
interstellar medium towards Sgr B2(M), future work to improve
our line lists for these isotopologues would seem to be worthwhile.
More laboratory work to create experimental line lists for
 \ and \ would enable the MARVEL procedure to be applied;
PES refinement should follow for both cases, allowing improved
line lists to be calculated. 

 obtain \ abundance ratios for
seventy-one field red giants and identify cases where 
 . The relative contribution to stellar interior 
opacity, and therefore to stellar structure, by isotopologues having 
one or more substituted by a atom, 
will become increasingly important as the \ ratio decreases.
Additional line lists for the remaining isotopologues
( , \ and ) could be computed should the need arise.

Conclusions

We have calculated new "AtLast"\ line lists for the electronic ground
state of the \ molecule and its two -substituted
isotopologues, known to be important in astrophysics. We have made the
transition, states and partition function files available through the
ExoMol database. Our line lists are applicable to studies of the
interstellar medium as well as cool star and planetary atmospheres.
In the \ case, the MARVEL procedure has provided experimental
accuracy in all comparisons; the other two line lists are not as
accurate, and improvements to be made in the future have been
identified.

Data Availability

As with other ExoMol line lists, our energy levels (states file),
line lists in the form of Einstein A-Coefficients (trans file), 
calculated partition functions and opacities are available in the
ExoMol Database (www.exomol.com).
Our refined version of the PES for
 \ has been included in tabular form, along with a
 FORTRAN90 subroutine for reading it,
as supplementary material. 

Supplementary Material

's PES has been
refined using experimental energy levels through the MARVEL procedure.
 [their table 3] 2016JChPh.144d4307S provide non-redundant
parameters which, when used with their equation 3.1, defines the PES
they publish. Lines of the file

 

list our revisions to their non-redundant parameters; in the preceding lines,
the number of data records, equilibrium bond-lengths in \ and the
equilibrium bond-angle supplement in degrees, have been provided.
With the additional file

 

we have presented a FORTRAN90 program which may be compiled
and used following

 

to compute potential energies in \ for the bond-angles and
bond-lengths listed in Lines of the input file;
 \ potential energies listed in the same lines should
agree with those computed to the digit.

mnras
